questions.promptTex: \text{Encrypt the word } HELLO \text{ with Caesar shift 3.} \\ \text{Enter the result (in capital letters, no spaces).}
hints: Shift each letter forward by 3: H→K, E→H, L→O, L→O, O→R.
questions.explanation: $H + 3 = K, E + 3 = H, L + 3 = O, L + 3 = O, O + 3 = R$. Encrypted: KHOOR.
M→R, A→F, T→Y, H→M. Result: RFYM.

questions.promptTex: \text{Decrypt } QHHG \text{ with shift 3 (i.e., shift back by 3).}
hints: Apply shift $-3$ to each letter, with mod 26 to wrap if you go below A.
questions.explanation: Shift each letter back by 3: Q→N, H→E, H→E, G→D. Result: NEED.

steps.text: **Recognize the recipe.** Decryption is the inverse of encryption: shift each letter BACKWARDS by 3 (i.e., apply shift $-3$ mod 26).

**Why mod-26 matters in general.** If a letter index would go negative (e.g., A=0 shifted by $-3$ gives $-3$), add 26 to wrap: $-3 + 26 = 23 = X$. The Caesar cipher *is* modular arithmetic on the alphabet.

questions.promptTex: \text{Letter } X \text{ (= 23) shifted by } 7 \text{ becomes which letter (give 0-25 number)?}

questions.prompt: Why is the Caesar cipher easily broken?
choices: There are only 25 possible shifts — brute force works.
It uses letters of the alphabet.
questions.explanation: Only 25 possible keys — brute force is trivial. Modern ciphers use astronomically large key spaces (e.g., $2^{256}$ keys for AES-256) to defeat brute force.

questions.promptTex: \text{Shift 13 (ROT13) applied twice to letter } G \text{ gives:}
hints: Back to G. G is letter 6.
questions.explanation: ROT13 applied twice = shift by 26 = identity. So $G \to G$ (number 6).

steps.text: **Compose the two shifts.** Applying ROT13 twice is equivalent to a single shift of $13 + 13 = 26$.
**A shift of 26 is the IDENTITY mod 26.** $26 \bmod 26 = 0$ — every letter returns to itself.
**So ROT13 is its OWN INVERSE.** Apply it once to encrypt, apply it AGAIN to decrypt. Same operation.
**For G specifically.** G has index 6. After ROT13 twice: still 6 = G.

choices: It's the only shift that's its own inverse (ROT13 ∘ ROT13 = identity).
questions.explanation: 13 + 13 = 26 ≡ 0, so two applications of ROT13 cancel out. This makes ROT13 a self-inverse — same operation to encrypt and decrypt. Popular for casual obfuscation on the early internet.

questions.promptTex: \text{Letter } Z \text{ (= 25) shifted by } -3 \text{ becomes which letter (0-25)?}
